title: Gastrolobium sericeum
intro: Gastrolobium sericeum, also known as the Woolly Gastrolobium or Sticky Pear, is a flowering plant native to Western Australia, characterized by its distinctive, woolly leaves and clusters of yellow or orange flowers. The plant has been traditionally used in Australian Aboriginal medicine, and its extracts have been found to possess health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in wound healing and the treatment of infections. Bioactive constituents of Gastrolobium sericeum include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The plant is commonly prepared as a tincture, infusion, or decoction, and is used in traditional herbal remedies to support immune function, reduce inflammation, and promote wound healing.
taxonomy: Gastrolobium sericeum is a species of flowering plant in the pea family Fabaceae. It is native to Western Australia and is classified as a shrub. Gastrolobium sericeum is further classified as a member of the genus Gastrolobium, which is a group of flowering plants that are commonly known as poison peas due to their toxic seeds. These seeds contain the toxin glycoside, which makes the plant poisonous to humans and animals if ingested. As a member of the Fabaceae family, Gastrolobium sericeum is closely related to other flowering plants such as beans and peas.

side_effects: Gastrolobium sericeum is a toxic plant native to Western Australia. When ingested, it can cause gastrointestinal symptoms, such as nausea and vomiting. Ingestion of the plant can also lead to abdominal pain and diarrhea. The plant's toxicity is due to the presence of toxic compounds called pyrrolizidine alkaloids. Ingestion of even small amounts of the plant can be hazardous, and medical attention should be sought immediately if exposure occurs.
